resume:
PROGRAM MANAGER, ACADEMIC TECHNOLOGY & STUDENT SUCCESS
Lakeview Community College District

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
I am a mid-career program manager working at the intersection of academic technology, faculty development, and student-success analytics within a multi-campus community college district. Over the past several years I have managed the rollout of a new learning management system, coordinated a corequisite-remediation redesign in developmental math, and stood up an early-alert program intended to surface at-risk students earlier in the term. My day-to-day work involves translating between three audiences who rarely speak the same language: faculty who care about pedagogy and academic freedom, IT staff who care about integrations and uptime, and institutional-research analysts who care about clean longitudinal data. I see my core skill as sequencing complex, multi-stakeholder initiatives so that the technical work, the faculty change-management work, and the measurement work all land in a coherent order rather than colliding. I am comfortable in committee rooms and in vendor negotiations, and I am the person who keeps the project plan honest when a dozen stakeholders each remember the timeline differently.

EXPERIENCE

Lakeview Community College District — Program Manager, Academic Technology & Student Success (2019 to present)
I manage a portfolio of three interlocking initiatives across four campuses serving roughly 22,000 credit students. The first is the district-wide migration of our learning management system from a legacy self-hosted platform to a cloud-hosted LMS, a project that touched every instructor and required a faculty-training program, a course-migration effort, and a phased cutover by term. The second is a corequisite-remediation redesign in developmental mathematics, in which we replaced standalone remedial sequences with credit-bearing college math paired with a mandatory support lab, in partnership with the math faculty senate. The third is an early-alert and case-management program, in which faculty submit progress flags that route to advisors for outreach. My responsibilities span project planning, vendor management, grant reporting, faculty professional-development design, and the measurement plans that tell us whether any of this moved completion and persistence.

Selected work and outcomes:
- Led the LMS migration across four campuses. Built the migration runbook, the rollback criteria, and the term-by-term cutover schedule. Coordinated a faculty-readiness program of workshops, a sandbox period, and a course-template library so instructors were not starting from a blank shell.
- Coordinated the corequisite redesign with the math department. This involved restructuring course catalogs, retraining advisors on placement, and aligning the support-lab staffing model with enrollment. Throughput in college-level math in the first year improved relative to the old standalone-remediation baseline, and we tracked persistence into the next math course.
- Stood up the early-alert program. Designed the flag taxonomy with faculty, configured the routing rules with IT, and built the advisor case-management workflow. Reported on flag volume, outreach rates, and the share of flagged students who re-engaged.
- Managed two grant-reporting cycles tied to a state student-success grant, including the data appendices that the institutional-research office and I assembled jointly.
- Chaired a cross-functional steering committee with representation from faculty senate, IT, advising, and institutional research, and ran the change-control process when scope or sequence shifted.

Lakeview Community College District — Instructional Technologist (2016 to 2019)
Before moving into program management I was an instructional technologist supporting faculty on the legacy LMS. I ran the help desk escalation queue for course-tools issues, built training materials, and supported the pilot cohorts for early online and hybrid course development. I was the person faculty called when a gradebook broke the night before grades were due. This role gave me the credibility with faculty that later made the migration and the corequisite work possible, because instructors knew I understood their classrooms and not just the software.

Lakeview Community College District — Academic Department Coordinator, Mathematics & Sciences (2013 to 2016)
I coordinated scheduling, adjunct onboarding, and assessment-data collection for the math and science division. I learned how course catalogs, prerequisite chains, and placement policies actually work in practice, which is the unglamorous foundation that the later corequisite redesign depended on. I also supported program-review cycles, which is where I first learned to assemble longitudinal student-outcome data for accreditation.

APPROACH AND PHILOSOPHY
I believe most academic-technology projects fail not on the technology but on the sequencing and the change management. If you flip the cutover before faculty are trained, you get a revolt; if you train before the platform is stable, you waste goodwill. If you launch early-alert flags before advisors have the capacity to act on them, you train faculty that flagging does nothing. My job is to order the pieces so each one is ready when the next depends on it, and to keep an honest record of what actually happened and when, because in a district this size everyone reconstructs the timeline to suit their own narrative. I am careful, I document decisions, and I try to make the measurement plan a first-class part of the project rather than an afterthought we bolt on at grant-reporting time.

SKILLS
Program and project management for academic initiatives; LMS administration and migration planning; faculty professional-development design; corequisite and developmental-education redesign; early-alert and case-management configuration; student-success and persistence analytics; grant reporting; cross-functional facilitation; vendor management.

EDUCATION AND CERTIFICATIONS
M.Ed., Higher Education Administration. B.A., Mathematics. Project Management Professional (PMP). Certified in two major learning-management platforms and in an early-alert case-management system.
jd: Lakeview Community College District is hiring a Program Manager for Academic Technology & Student Success to own the sequencing of our next wave of student-success initiatives across four campuses. The immediate mandate is to coordinate three overlapping efforts — completing the cloud LMS migration, expanding the corequisite-math redesign to additional disciplines, and maturing the early-alert program from pilot to scale — without letting them collide during the academic calendar. You will own the integrated project plan, the change-control process, and the measurement plan that reports completion, persistence, and equity-gap metrics to the district board and the state grant office. We need a manager who is rigorous about chronology: who can say exactly which initiative launched in which term, what depended on what, and why a given sequence was chosen. You will facilitate a steering committee spanning faculty senate, IT, advising, and institutional research, and you will be the single source of truth when stakeholders remember the timeline differently. Reports to the Dean of Academic Affairs.

history.q: Give me the shape of your portfolio at Lakeview — the three initiatives and the campuses they span.
Let's start with the LMS migration. What was the cutover model — big-bang or phased?
Which campus or term went first on the LMS cutover?
How did you sequence faculty training relative to the cutover?
Now the corequisite redesign. When did that launch relative to the LMS work?
What was the throughput baseline in college math before the corequisite change?
And the early-alert program — pilot term and scale term?
Did early-alert depend on the LMS migration being done, or were they independent?
Walk me through which term each of the three initiatives first went live.
You said the corequisite launch was before the LMS cutover completed — is that right?
Then how did advisors do placement retraining without the new LMS in place?
Let's pin the grant cycle. Which initiative was the grant reporting tied to first?
So in the first grant year, which initiatives actually had data to report?
Did the corequisite throughput result come from before or after the early-alert launch?
I'm hearing different orderings. Can you fix the sequence: LMS cutover, corequisite launch, early-alert pilot — which was first, second, third?
And where does the steering committee's change-control process fit on that timeline?
Take me through the full timeline one term at a time, start to finish — when did each initiative go live and what depended on what?

candidate_last_answer: Right, term by term. So the LMS cutover started in fall — the first campus went live that fall, and the corequisite redesign launched the same term, actually I think the corequisite math went first because the faculty senate approved it earlier, in the spring before. But the throughput number I gave you, that was from the year the early-alert program was already running, so early-alert must have come first, before the corequisite launch. Except early-alert depended on the new LMS routing, which wasn't finished until after the cutover completed across all four campuses, which was the following year. So the corequisite result and the early-alert flags were really happening at the same time as the migration, more or less, and the grant report covered all of it in year one even though some of it hadn't fully launched yet.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: early-alert must have come first, before the corequisite launch. Except early-alert depended on the new LMS routing, which wasn't finished until after the cutover completed

expected-yield: a single internally-consistent term-by-term ordering of the LMS cutover, corequisite launch, and early-alert pilot that resolves the dependency contradiction (early-alert needing LMS routing yet supposedly preceding the corequisite launch) and ties the throughput number to a specific term
